This is a central evidence-claim mismatch: a human-rated dialogue-quality score over 300 dialogues is not a measurement of iterate convergence, and no convergence-curve or gradient-norm data is reported.","R1.4":"The entire novelty claim rests on beating Polyak (1967). If that baseline is misattributed or superseded by post-2010 nonconvex mirror-descent literature (e.g., Duchi & Hazan, Reddi et al.), the claimed improvement may be neither novel nor the relevant state of the art.","R1.7":"Citing Polyak (1967) as establishing an O(1/T^{1/4}) rate for nonsmooth nonconvex optimization on convex compact sets is likely inaccurate — Polyak's 1967 subgradient analysis targets convex objectives. This citation underpins the core novelty claim, making it a load-bearing attribution error."},"clarification_needed":true,"clarification_question":"Should this review prioritize technical correctness (logical validity of theoretical claims, evidence-claim alignment, citation accuracy) or writing quality (prose clarity, paragraph structure, style)?"}

rationale.R1.1: The artifact advances strong first-in-class contribution claims ('first sublinear-rate guarantee for memory-retrieval policy optimization') requiring reconstruction and verification of the stated thesis and gap statement.
rationale.R1.2: Multiple internal logical failures are present: (1) Polyak (1967) is a convex subgradient result — attributing an O(1/T^{1/4}) nonsmooth nonconvex rate to it is likely category-incorrect; (2) the inference that a Likert-score behavioral gain 'constitutes direct evidence of faster convergence' is a non sequitur — task performance does not proxy convergence rate; (3) the bounded-variance assumption invoked for the O(1/√T) proof is in tension with the article's own description of gradients as 'heavy-tailed.'
rationale.R1.3: The paper explicitly claims the BehaviorCoherence@K experiment validates the O(1/√T) optimization rate. This is a central evidence-claim mismatch: a human-rated dialogue-quality score over 300 dialogues is not a measurement of iterate convergence, and no convergence-curve or gradient-norm data is reported.
rationale.R1.4: The entire novelty claim rests on beating Polyak (1967). If that baseline is misattributed or superseded by post-2010 nonconvex mirror-descent literature (e.g., Duchi & Hazan, Reddi et al.), the claimed improvement may be neither novel nor the relevant state of the art.
rationale.R1.7: Citing Polyak (1967) as establishing an O(1/T^{1/4}) rate for nonsmooth nonconvex optimization on convex compact sets is likely inaccurate — Polyak's 1967 subgradient analysis targets convex objectives. This citation underpins the core novelty claim, making it a load-bearing attribution error.

parsed.clarification_question: Should this review prioritize technical correctness (logical validity of theoretical claims, evidence-claim alignment, citation accuracy) or writing quality (prose clarity, paragraph structure, style)?
